perturbation.original_text: Buyer shall keep the Confidential Materials confidential and shall not disclose any of the Confidential Materials in any manner whatsoever, provided, however, that (i) Buyer may make any disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Materials to which Owner gives its prior written consent; and (ii) any information contained in the Confidential Materials may be disclosed to Buyer’s Representatives who need to know that information for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction with Owner and who agree to keep that information confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer shall keep the Confidential Materials confidential and shall not disclose any of the Confidential Materials in any manner whatsoever, provided, however, that (i) Buyer may make any disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Materials to which Owner gives its prior written consent; and (ii) any information contained in the Confidential Materials may be disclosed to Buyer’s Representatives who need to know that information for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction with Owner, regardless of whether they agree to keep that information confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for Buyer's Representatives to agree to confidentiality before receiving confidential materials creates a legal contradiction, potentially violating trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Disclosing trade secrets to representatives without ensuring they agree to confidentiality undermines these reasonable efforts, potentially nullifying trade secret protection under California law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for Buyer's Representatives to agree to confidentiality, which contradicts the need to maintain secrecy of trade secrets as per CUTSA. The Law Explanation states that disclosing trade secrets to representatives without ensuring they agree to confidentiality undermines reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines 'improper means' to include 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By removing the requirement for Buyer's Representatives to agree to confidentiality, the modified contract facilitates a potential breach of the duty to maintain secrecy, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer shall reimburse Owner for all costs and expenses, including reasonable attorney’s fees, incurred by Owner if Owner successfully enforces the obligations of Buyer and its Representatives hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer shall reimburse Owner for all costs and expenses, including attorney’s fees limited to $500, incurred by Owner if Owner successfully enforces the obligations of Buyer and its Representatives hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting attorney's fees to $500 regardless of the actual fees incurred may contradict California's principle of full compensation, potentially affecting the owner's ability to fully recover losses.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1033.5(a)(10)
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally allows for the recovery of reasonable attorney's fees as part of costs in certain actions. Capping fees at $500 may be deemed unreasonable, especially if the actual fees are significantly higher, hindering full compensation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1033.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1033.5. (a) The following items are allowable as costs under Section 1032: (1) Filing, motion, and jury fees. (2) Juror food and lodging while they are kept together during trial and after the jury retires for deliberation. (3) (A) Taking, video recording, and transcribing necessary depositions, including an original and one copy of those taken by the claimant and one copy of depositions taken by the party against whom costs are allowed. (B) Fees of a certified or registered interpreter for the deposition of a party or witness who does not proficiently speak or understand the English language. (C) Travel expenses to attend depositions. (4) Service of process by a public officer, registered process server, or other means, as follows: (A) When service is by a public officer, the recoverable cost is the fee authorized by law at the time of service. (B) If service is by a process server registered pursuant to Chapter 16 (commencing with Section 22350) of Division 8 of the Business and Professions Code, the recoverable cost is the amount actually i
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by limiting attorney's fees to $500, creates a legal contradiction under the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' category. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest that this limit may contradict California's principle of full compensation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 1033.5 (a), indicates that certain costs are allowable under Section 1032, suggesting a framework for cost recovery, which could include attorney's fees. Limiting these fees to a fixed amount, regardless of actual expenses, could undermine the principle of full compensation, potentially making the contract unenforceable regarding attorney's fees beyond $500.

perturbation.original_text: In the event the sale of the Property is consummated and the Buyer represented by a Buyer’s Broker as disclosed herein, TKG shall pay to Buyer’s Broker per a separate agreement out of the commissions received by TKG from Owner.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event the sale of the Property is consummated and the Buyer represented by a Buyer’s Broker as disclosed herein, TKG shall pay to Buyer’s Broker per a separate agreement out of the commissions received by TKG from Owner, but only if Buyer's Broker has no prior violations.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a 'no prior violations' contingency for the buyer's broker to receive commission, without defining 'violations,' introduces ambiguity and may violate fair commission practices.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 10176
perturbation.law_explanation: While 10176 generally talks about disciplinary action, arbitrarily withholding commission because of 'prior violations' not specified could be seen as acting in a way that is against what is considered fair and ethical business practice. This creates an unagreed upon condition that violates fair trade commission practices.

REAL ESTATE [10000 - 11506] ( Division 4 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) PART 1. LICENSING OF PERSONS [10000 - 10580] ( Part 1 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) CHAPTER 3. Real Estate Regulations [10130 - 10249.93] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) ARTICLE 3. Disciplinary Action [10175 - 10186.9] ( Article 3 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) 10176. The commissioner may, upon his or her own motion, and shall, upon the verified complaint in writing of any person, investigate the actions of any person engaged in the business or acting in the capacity of a real estate licensee within this state, and he or she may temporarily suspend or permanently revoke a real estate license at any time where the licensee, while a real estate licensee, in performing or attempting to perform any of the acts within the scope of this chapter has been guilty of any of the following: (a) Making any substantial misrepresentation. (b) Making any false promises of a character likely to influence, persuade, or induce. (c) A continued and flagrant course of misrepresentation or making of false promises through licensees. (d) Acting for more than one party in a transaction without the knowledge or consent of all parties thereto. (e) Commingling with
perturbation.justification: The original contract states TKG shall pay the Buyer's Broker commissions received from the Owner. The changed text adds a contingency that TKG will only pay if the Buyer's Broker has no prior violations. This creates a contradiction because it introduces an arbitrary condition not initially agreed upon, potentially violating fair commission practices. The Law Explanation supports this by saying arbitrarily withholding commission because of 'prior violations' could be seen as acting in a way that is against what is considered fair and ethical business practice. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses disciplinary actions against real estate licensees, highlighting the commissioner's power to investigate actions and suspend/revoke licenses. While this snippet doesn't directly address commission disputes, it suggests that actions affecting a licensee's ability to conduct business are subject to regulatory scrutiny. The changed text gives TKG power to determine 'violations' and withhold payment, creating a situation where fair commission practices could be undermined.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer may not assign this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer may assign this Agreement to any entity they choose, at any time, without notice or consent from the Owner.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing unrestricted assignment contradicts the common law principle that contracts are generally not freely assignable if they involve personal services or if the assignment would materially alter the other party's obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 1457 is about burdens, the general implication is that contracts cannot be freely assigned if it would materially alter the original agreement. Allowing the Buyer to assign to 'any entity they choose' impacts the contract substantially.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1457. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 1. OBLIGATIONS IN GENERAL [1427 - 1543] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. TRANSFER OF OBLIGATIONS [1457 - 1471] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1457. The burden of an obligation may be transferred with the consent of the party entitled to its benefit, but not otherwise, except as provided by Section 1466. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original text prohibited assignment, whereas the changed text allows the buyer to assign the agreement to any entity without notice or consent. This contradicts California Civil Code Section 1457, which states that 'The burden of an obligation may be transferred with the consent of the party entitled to its benefit, but not otherwise...' The changed text removes the requirement for consent, directly contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement of the parties concerning its subject matter. There are no promises or other agreements, oral or written, express or implied, between them other than as set forth in this Agreement concerning the subject matter of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement of the parties concerning its subject matter, except for any oral agreements made separately that both parties have forgotten.
perturbation.explanation: Adding an exception for 'forgotten oral agreements' directly contradicts the purpose of an integration clause and violates the parol evidence rule.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1856
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1856 codifies the Parol Evidence Rule, which generally prevents the introduction of extrinsic evidence (like prior oral agreements) to contradict or vary the terms of a written agreement intended to be the complete and exclusive statement of the parties' agreement. The modified text attempts to allow exactly this, which the Parol Evidence Rule exists to disallow.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1856. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the parol evidence rule, as codified in CCP 1856(a), which states that terms in a writing intended as a final expression of agreement cannot be contradicted by evidence of prior or contemporaneous oral agreements. The change makes an exception for 'forgotten oral agreements,' which seeks to allow exactly what the rule prohibits: the introduction of extrinsic evidence (oral agreements) to alter the terms of a written contract intended to be the complete agreement. This undermines the integrity and enforceability of the written agreement, hence creating a legal contradiction.
